text:
[3. Wording your message > 3.1 Style > Put the reader centre stage]

Put the reader centre stage

All information must be correct and clear, and must not be misleading. Specific rules apply to sustainability claims to prevent greenwashing. Sustainability claims are all expressions relating to the sustainability of products, services or business operations in terms of their environmental, social or governance (ESG) aspects. These claims must be accurate, representative, specific, substantiated and up to date. In addition, the information must be understandable, appropriate and easy to find. To align with this, we follow the AFM’s Guidelines on Sustainability Claims. See Ruby (Confluence) for more information and examples (in Dutch). 

Don’t write about internal processes or products, as that will not interest your readers. Put yourself in your readers’ shoes and anticipate their questions. Why is this message useful for _them_ ? 

**12** 

**Writing Guide • January 2026** 

**Wording your message** 

Question-and-answer constructions are often used in Dutch but are less common in English and should be avoided, as they can sound unnatural. 

‘If you suddenly need money, you can overdraw your Privélimiet Plus account.’ 

‘Your washing machine always seems to break at the worst time – when you’ve just been on an expensive holiday, for example. Our Privélimiet Plus account lets you overdraw, so you can replace your washing machine right away.’

metadata.breadcrumb: 3. Wording your message > 3.1 Style > Put the reader centre stage

text:
[4. Accuracy > 4.1 Standards for spelling and vocabulary > When do we use capital letters? ABN AMRO’s standards are listed below:]

When do we use capital letters? ABN AMRO’s standards are listed below:

## **When do we use capital letters? ABN AMRO’s standards are listed below:**

- **Proper nouns:** when proper nouns are capitalised in English, only significant words 

   - in the heading are capitalised (not short words such as ‘of’ and ‘the’). Examples are the University of Amsterdam, the Secretary of State. 

- 

   - **Department names:** always capitalise, for example Group Compliance, Group Sustainable Development, Press Office. However, when referring to the business area or market segment, no capitals are needed, as in: ‘Group Sustainable Development concentrates on **sustainable development** .’ 

- 

   - **Government and politics:** organisations, ministries, departments, treaties, acts, etc., generally take capital letters when their full name is used. For example: Ministry of Finance, Health and Safety at Work Act. Do not capitalise descriptions: the French **parliament** , the American , etc. The full names of political **finance department** 

   - parties take capitals: Labour Party, Republican Party. Their members are also capitalised: Liberal Democrats, Republicans, etc. 

- 

**24** 

**Writing Guide • January 2026** 

**Accuracy** 

- **Months and days of the week:** these always begin with a capital letter in English, as in: Sunday 10 November 2014. 

- **People:** no general rules apply to ranks and titles, but as a rule of thumb, capitalise when written with a name. Examples: President Obama, King Charles, the Duke of Edinburgh. 

   - **Abbreviations:** when shortened from more than one word, abbreviations are usually capitalised (PDF, AI, NGO). Note that lower case is used for measures (kg, km, mph). Acronyms are capitalised, for example UNESCO. Also see chapter 4, ‘Abbreviations’. 

-

**Clients could ask a range of questions:**

text:
[1. Prepare your text > 1.1 Define your target audience: who are you writing for? > Objective:]

Objective:

**I want to:** inform instruct convince 

the reader will know [...] the reader will be able to [...] the reader will think that [...] the reader will take action [...] the reader will feel [...] 

set something in motion create a feeling 

Texts often have more than one objective, and that’s fine. But be careful not to cram too many messages into your text or it may become confusing. It should always be clear to your readers what they need to do after reading your text. 

- **How does the reader feel about the subject?** 

If you are communicating a negative or sensitive message, explain it carefully and show that you understand the reader and their point of view. 

- **What are the characteristics of your target audience?** 

What is their average age, for example? You should use a different tone to address young people than an older audience. Young people are also more likely to be familiar with the online possibilities we offer.
